projects.title: DocuSense — Document Intent Classifier + LLM Reasoning (Azure ML)
projects.value: A two-route document intelligence service on Azure ML managed endpoints — a millisecond-scale LightGBM intent classifier handles 80% of traffic without calling GPT, while the reasoning route chains PII scrub, hybrid RAG retrieval, tool calling, structured outputs, and citation/safety guardrails through GPT-5.1, with LLM-as-judge evals running nightly to catch prompt regressions before they reach production.
projects.architecture: POST /classify → LightGBM head (embeddings) → fast route | POST /reason → Presidio PII scrub → Azure AI Search (hybrid vector + keyword) → GPT-5.1 (tool calling + json_schema structured output) → citation guardrail + Azure Content Safety → response | AML Training Pipeline: blob storage → train classifier → evaluate (gate on F1) → register model | Nightly: App Insights traces → LLM-as-judge (rubric scorer) → baseline comparison → GitHub issue on regression
decisions: Fast + slow routing with confidence threshold — LightGBM over hashed bag-of-words embeddings resolves 80% of requests in <5ms without an LLM call; only documents below the confidence gate escalate to the GPT reasoning path, cutting per-request cost by 10×.

Structured outputs via json_schema response_format — the ReasoningResponse Pydantic model generates the OpenAI JSON schema at build time, guaranteeing parseable responses with zero regex extraction; the same model serves as the API contract and the LLM constraint.

Positional args in AML components — AML strips --flag names that collide with declared input/output names; all pipeline entrypoints use typer.Argument (positional) and accept booleans as strings to survive AML's command rewriting.

Semantic ranker as opt-in, not default — Azure AI Search semantic ranker requires Standard tier and explicit index configuration; the retriever defaults to pure vector + keyword hybrid which works on any tier, with semantic ranking enabled only when explicitly configured.

ScriptedFakeLLM for deterministic CI — every test and eval uses a fake LLM with scripted responses, keeping the full suite offline and under 3 seconds; real Azure OpenAI is only invoked in the nightly prompt regression workflow with explicit cost controls.

Credential injection at submit/deploy time — neither pipeline containers nor endpoint containers carry .env files; submit_training_job.py and deploy_endpoint.py read local .env via pydantic-settings and inject credentials as environment variables, keeping secrets out of YAML and Git.

LLM-as-judge with baseline comparison — the Judge class scores responses on 4 rubric dimensions (1–5), compares the mean against a committed baseline JSON, and fails CI if the delta exceeds a configurable threshold; local mode uses a fake judge for zero-cost iteration.

projects.title: SignalFlow — Predictive Maintenance MLOps on Azure
projects.value: An end-to-end MLOps platform that trains a binary failure-risk classifier on industrial sensor telemetry, registers versions in Azure ML Model Registry with pipeline-gated quality checks, deploys behind a managed online endpoint with blue/green traffic shifting, and monitors data drift on a schedule — all provisioned by Terraform and driven by GitHub Actions, cutting the path from experiment to production deployment to a single `git tag`.
projects.architecture: Sensor Parquet → Azure ML Pipeline (prepare → train → gate) → Model Registry (PR-AUC gated) → Managed Online Endpoint (blue/green) → FastAPI + vanilla JS Dashboard (local/Azure toggle) · Evidently drift monitor → App Insights + GitHub Issue · Terraform (workspace, storage, ACR, Key Vault, Log Analytics, compute cluster)
decisions: Positional arguments over --flag options in AML components — Azure ML strips flag names that collide with declared input/output names; using typer.Argument (positional) is the only reliable pattern for component CLIs.

Single feature implementation shared by training and serving — `build_feature_frame()` is called identically in the pipeline train step and the online scoring script, eliminating training/serving skew by construction rather than by convention.

PR-AUC threshold gate instead of accuracy — class imbalance (rare failures) makes accuracy misleading; the pipeline gate step rejects models below a PR-AUC floor and tags every registered version with recall-at-precision-90.
PYTHONPATH wrapper over pip install in AML — `aml_run.sh` sets PYTHONPATH=src so the signalflow package is importable without building a wheel, keeping component images cacheable and iteration fast.

Skip mlflow.log_artifact when tracking_uri is azureml:// — AML auto-captures outputs via component mount points; calling log_artifact triggers a conflicting azureml_artifacts_builder path that breaks with mlflow-skinny pre-installed in AML images.

Server-side proxy for Azure endpoint in local frontend — Azure ML managed endpoints don't return CORS headers; the FastAPI server exposes /proxy/score that forwards requests server-side via httpx, letting the same vanilla JS dashboard score against both local and cloud models.

Fallback model path resolution in score.py — registered models from the pipeline have a nested model/ folder while local models sit at root; init() checks both paths, making the same scoring script work across registration methods without reconfiguration.

Soft-delete-aware Terraform lifecycle — Azure ML workspaces enter soft-delete on destroy; the infra docs and CI include a forceToPurge REST call before re-provisioning, preventing the opaque 'Soft-deleted workspace exists' error on subsequent applies.

projects.title: Real-Time IoT Anomaly Detection Pipeline
projects.value: Detects equipment anomalies in real time from streaming sensor data, before they become costly failures.
projects.architecture: Sensor simulator → Kafka (partitioned by device) → Spark Structured Streaming → Delta Lake (bronze / silver / gold) → Alert dashboard
decisions: Kafka over a simpler queue — needed ordered delivery per device and the ability to replay historical streams for backtesting alert thresholds.
Structured Streaming over batch — sub-minute detection latency is the entire point; a 15-minute batch window would defeat the purpose.
Medallion architecture in Delta Lake — raw events preserved untouched in bronze for replay/debugging, cleaned in silver, aggregated alerts in gold.

projects.title: Self-Healing Daily Pipeline with Airflow
projects.value: A daily ingestion pipeline that catches bad data before it reaches dashboards — and explains exactly what broke and why.
projects.architecture: Source API → Airflow DAG (extract → validate → load) → Data quality gate (pass → warehouse table / fail → quarantine + Slack alert)
decisions: Airflow over cron — needed dependency management, automatic retries with backoff, and the ability to backfill any historical date on demand.
Quarantine on failure, not fail-fast — bad batches are isolated for inspection rather than blocking the whole pipeline, so good data still flows.
Idempotent task design — every run for a given date can be safely re-executed without creating duplicates, which is what makes backfills safe.

projects.title: Agentic Root-Cause Investigator for the Self-Healing Pipeline
projects.value: When bad data lands in quarantine, an LLM-powered agent investigates why — pulling its own evidence from logs, validation results, and schema diffs — and ships a structured incident report to Slack without a human opening a single tab.
projects.architecture: Quarantine event → Airflow task (trigger_rule=all_done) → pre-check: quarantine count == 0 → skip / > 0 → Groq tool-calling agent → evidence tools (quarantine table, GE results, Airflow logs, schema diff) → structured JSON verdict → Markdown report + Slack alert
decisions: Read-only tools, no auto-remediation — the agent diagnoses and reports; a human always owns the fix. Letting an LLM write to production tables without an approval gate is the kind of decision that's easy to regret.
Cheap pre-check before every LLM call — quarantine count is queried first so clean runs never spend an API call, keeping the demo cost-free at scale.

Hard iteration cap with a circuit breaker — the tool-calling loop raises InvestigationError rather than spinning indefinitely if the model can't converge, same discipline as any retriable production operation.

projects.title: Analytics Engineering with dbt + Snowflake
projects.value: Transforms raw operational data into trusted, tested business metrics — with full lineage so any number can be traced back to its source.
projects.architecture: Raw source tables → Snowflake → dbt (staging → intermediate → marts) → tested, documented models → BI tool
decisions: Layered modeling (staging / intermediate / marts) — keeps raw-to-business-logic transformations traceable and each layer independently testable.
dbt tests as the contract — not_null, relationships, and custom business logic tests run on every build, so broken assumptions fail loudly, not silently in a dashboard.
Incremental models for high-volume tables — avoids full table rebuilds, keeping both cost and run time bounded as data grows.

projects.title: NL-to-SQL Agent over dbt Marts
projects.value: Lets anyone query trusted dbt marts in plain English — the agent grounds itself in dbt's own schema metadata, validates every query before it touches a database, and self-corrects when it gets the SQL wrong.
projects.architecture: Plain-English question → Groq agent → SQL generated against dbt manifest schema → sqlglot guard (read-only, catalog-bound, row-limited) → DuckDB demo / Snowflake prod → self-correction on failure → result + attempt trail
decisions: Schema grounding from dbt's own manifest.json, not a hand-maintained file — the agent's vocabulary can never drift further from reality than the last `dbt docs generate`.
Read-only SQL guard via sqlglot — every candidate query is parsed and rejected unless it's a single SELECT against catalog-approved tables, before it ever reaches a database.

Bounded self-correction loop — failed validation or execution feeds the real error back to the model for up to 3 attempts, surfaced in the UI as an attempt trail rather than hidden behind the final answer.

projects.title: Serverless Data Lakehouse on AWS
projects.value: Ingests a live API into a queryable S3 data lake — Glue handles schema and transformation, Redshift Spectrum queries the lake directly with no load step.
projects.architecture: API → Lambda (ingest) → S3 raw/ → Glue Crawler → Glue Catalog → Glue ETL (Spark) → S3 processed/ (Parquet) → Glue Crawler → Redshift Spectrum → API Gateway → Front
decisions: Right tool per stage — Lambda for cheap scheduled ingestion, Glue (Spark) for bulk transformation and schema management. Each service does what it's good at, not duplicating the other.
Glue Workflow for orchestration — crawl(raw) → transform → crawl(processed) chained with conditional triggers, so the pipeline is decoupled from Lambda and fully Glue-native.

Redshift Spectrum over the Glue Catalog — the warehouse queries Parquet in S3 directly as external tables, avoiding a COPY/load step and any Glue↔Redshift networking, with IAM as the only integration surface.

Terraform for the whole stack — S3, Lambda, Glue (catalog + crawlers + job + workflow), Redshift Serverless and its Spectrum IAM role all provisioned and destroyed from one IaC tree, keeping cost bounded between demos.

projects.title: Clinical Trial Signal Detection (Real-Time Pharmacovigilance)
projects.value: A real-time pharmacovigilance pipeline that monitors adverse event streams across clinical trial sites — Event Hub ingests reports, Stream Analytics applies tumbling-window detection in seconds, Synapse SQL Pool persists signals, and a FastAPI dashboard surfaces safety alerts live, cutting signal detection latency from days to minutes.
projects.architecture: Trial Sites → Event Hub (adverse-events) → Stream Analytics (10-min tumbling window, GROUP BY + HAVING) → Event Hub (signal-alerts) + ADLS Gen2 (bronze/) → Synapse Dedicated SQL Pool → FastAPI + vanilla JS Dashboard
decisions: Tumbling windows over sliding — produces non-overlapping, auditable signal periods that map 1:1 to regulatory dossier timelines; simpler to reason about in ASA SQL which lacks nested analytic functions.

Count threshold in ASA, incidence rate downstream — ASA SQL doesn't support nested aggregates or analytic functions over GROUP BY; the heavy math (rate per arm population) is computed in the Synapse gold layer and local detector for full accuracy.

Single GROUP BY + HAVING pattern in ASA — CTE-to-CTE JOINs on the same stream fail in ASA, NEWID() is unsupported, and SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(PARTITION BY) won't compile; a flat GROUP BY with CONCAT for synthetic IDs is the only reliable approach.

Synapse-first data loading with fixture fallback — API startup calls fetch_events() from Synapse; if data exists it serves real pipeline content, otherwise falls back to in-memory fixtures, enabling the same codebase to run locally at zero cost and in Azure with live data.

Dedicated SQL pool with no expression-based DEFAULTs — Synapse dedicated pools don't support DEFAULT GETUTCDATE() in DDL; columns use NULL and timestamps are set by the writer, which also avoids clock-skew issues between ASA and Synapse.

pyodbc import guard with graceful degradation — the Synapse client wraps pyodbc in try/except at module level (_PYODBC_AVAILABLE flag), so CI and local dev run fully in-memory without requiring ODBC driver installation.

Local detector mirrors ASA query — unit tests validate the same tumbling-window logic without an Azure dependency; 42 tests run in <1s with all Azure calls mocked via conftest monkeypatch of _SYNAPSE_AVAILABLE.

projects.title: Event-Driven Pipeline on GCP
projects.value: Streams live weather data through Cloud Functions into BigQuery — fully serverless, with Eventarc wiring so each stage triggers the next. A Cloud Run frontend displays the latest observation in real time.
projects.architecture: Open-Meteo API → Cloud Scheduler → Cloud Function (ingest) → Cloud Storage raw/ → Eventarc trigger → Cloud Function (transform) → BigQuery → Cloud Function (latest) → Cloud Run (frontend)
decisions: Cloud Functions Gen2 over Cloud Run for compute — the workload is small and bursty; Gen2 gives Eventarc integration and a 10-min timeout without container-management overhead.
Eventarc (object-finalize) for stage coupling — the transform function fires on new objects in GCS, so ingestion and transformation are decoupled; either can be replaced or retried independently.
BigQuery streaming inserts from the transform function — avoids a load job for single-row writes, keeping latency low while landing data in a warehouse with full SQL and BI tool support.
Cloud Run for the frontend — serves a static nginx container with the dashboard; zero-to-scale, no infrastructure to manage, and the `latest` Cloud Function provides the JSON API behind it.

Terraform for the full stack — GCS buckets, Cloud Functions, BigQuery dataset + table, Cloud Scheduler, Eventarc triggers, Cloud Run, Artifact Registry, and IAM — all provisioned and destroyed from one configuration.
uv + ruff for developer tooling — fast dependency resolution with lockfile, consistent formatting and linting across local dev and CI, zero-config via pyproject.toml.
Shared module pattern — ingest and transform logic lives in shared/ and is reused unchanged across AWS, Azure, and GCP implementations; a packaging script bundles it into each function zip.

projects.title: ADF-Orchestrated Pipeline on Azure
projects.value: Uses Azure Data Factory as the orchestration backbone — an HTTP Copy Activity lands raw data in ADLS Gen2, an Event Grid-triggered Azure Function transforms it, and Azure SQL serves it via a live dashboard on Static Web Apps, showing the native Azure integration pattern most teams actually use.
projects.architecture: API → ADF Pipeline (HTTP Copy Activity) → ADLS Gen2 raw/ → Event Grid → Azure Function (transform) → Azure SQL Database (serverless) → Static Web App Dashboard
decisions: Data Factory with HTTP connector — ADF's REST connector fails on gzip-encoded APIs like Open-Meteo, so the pipeline uses the HTTP connector (JsonSource + HttpReadSettings) which handles compression transparently.

ADLS Gen2 with hierarchical namespace — enables Synapse/Databricks compatibility, but requires Event Grid-based blob triggers instead of classic polling, which doesn't detect blobs on HNS-enabled accounts.

Event Grid system topic for blob trigger — wires BlobCreated events from the ADLS storage account to the Azure Function's webhook endpoint, replacing the unreliable polling trigger and enabling near-instant processing.
Azure SQL serverless with auto-pause — the warehouse costs nothing when idle (auto-pauses after 60 min), making it demo-safe while still being a real relational engine with full T-SQL support.

Terraform for infrastructure, CLI for ADF wiring — linked services, datasets, and pipelines are created via `az datafactory` CLI commands (reproducible and scriptable), while the Event Grid topic is created separately and must be deleted before `terraform destroy`.

Auto-wrapping in the transform function — ADF writes the raw API response directly, but the shared transform logic expects the wrapped envelope format from ingest.py, so the function detects and wraps automatically for compatibility.
Remote build for function deployment — `--build remote` ensures pip installs run against Azure's Python 3.12, avoiding ModuleNotFound errors from local/remote version mismatches.

projects.title: Airflow on Kubernetes — Orchestration Capstone
projects.value: Runs the same ingest + transform logic as the cloud-native projects, but you own the orchestration layer — showing when and why you'd choose a portable scheduler over vendor-specific triggers.
projects.architecture: Airflow (Helm on K8s) → Task: ingest pod → object storage → Task: transform pod → warehouse
decisions: kind/minikube over managed K8s — demonstrates the same Airflow-on-Kubernetes pattern without EKS/GKE/AKS cost; the Helm chart and manifests are portable to any cluster.
Same shared/ module as the cloud repos — the ingest and transform logic is identical, making the architectural comparison honest: only the orchestration and deployment layer changes.
PythonOperator first, KubernetesPodOperator as an extension point — starts simple, with a clear upgrade path to per-task isolation in its own pod for heavier workloads.
Warehouse-agnostic by design — the DAG can load into local Postgres (via the docker/ stack), or any of the three cloud warehouses via credentials passed as Kubernetes secrets.
